concepts.name: Variable & Expression
concepts.intuition_gloss: A letter standing in for a number you don't know yet, letting you write one sentence that covers infinitely many arithmetic facts at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: A symbol denoting an arbitrary or unknown element of a set, combined with constants and operations to form well-formed algebraic expressions.
sources: Khan Academy knowledge map: pre-algebra → algebra basics

concepts.name: Equation & Solving
concepts.intuition_gloss: A balanced scale of two expressions; solving means doing the same legal move to both sides until the unknown stands alone.
concepts.formal_gloss: An assertion that two expressions denote the same value; its solution set is the set of assignments to the variables making the assertion true, found by applying equivalence-preserving operations.

concepts.name: Linear Equation & Graph
concepts.intuition_gloss: The equation of steady, proportional change — its picture is always a straight line whose slope is the constant rate.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation of the form a1x1 + ... + anxn = b, degree one in each variable; in two variables its solution set is a line in the coordinate plane.

concepts.name: Inequality
concepts.intuition_gloss: An equation's looser cousin: instead of pinning a value down exactly, it fences off a whole region where the answer must live.
concepts.formal_gloss: A relation asserting one expression is less than (or at most) another under the order on the reals; solution sets are intervals or regions, and multiplying by negatives reverses the relation.

concepts.name: Quadratic Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: The first equation with a curve in it — a parabola — whose solutions are captured once and for all by a single closed formula.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a nonzero, solved by completing the square to give x = (-b ± sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / 2a, with the discriminant governing the nature of the roots.

concepts.name: Exponentials & Logarithms
concepts.intuition_gloss: Repeated multiplication grown up into a smooth machine for explosive growth, and its inverse, which converts multiplication into addition.
concepts.formal_gloss: The functions b^x, extended from integer exponents to the reals by the laws b^(x+y) = b^x b^y, and log_b as their inverse, satisfying log(xy) = log x + log y.

concepts.name: Factoring & Roots of Polynomials
concepts.intuition_gloss: Breaking a polynomial into simpler multiplied pieces, where each piece points to a place the graph crosses zero.
concepts.formal_gloss: Expressing a polynomial as a product of lower-degree polynomials; by the factor theorem, r is a root of p iff (x - r) divides p, so a degree-n polynomial has at most n roots.

concepts.name: Rational Expressions & Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: A fraction built out of polynomials — it behaves like ordinary fraction arithmetic until the bottom hits zero, where the graph tears open into holes and asymptotes.
concepts.formal_gloss: A quotient p(x)/q(x) of polynomials with q not the zero polynomial, defined wherever q(x) ≠ 0; added, multiplied, and simplified by the rules of fraction arithmetic, with zeros of q producing vertical asymptotes or removable holes.

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: Once you allow numbers in the plane, no polynomial equation is unsolvable — every one of degree n ≥ 1 has exactly n roots, counted with repeats.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every nonconstant polynomial with complex coefficients has a root in C; equivalently, C is algebraically closed, so every degree-n complex polynomial splits into n linear factors.

resources.title: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra — Numberphile (David Eisenbud)

sources: Princeton Companion V.13 (The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra)

concepts.name: Binary Operation & Algebraic Structure
concepts.intuition_gloss: Step back from numbers and ask what 'combining two things to get a third' can be in general — then classify worlds by which rules (associative, commutative, invertible) their combining obeys.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function from S × S to S; an algebraic structure is a set with one or more such operations satisfying specified axioms, the abstraction underlying groups, rings, and fields.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Subgroup & Lagrange's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A self-contained club of moves inside a bigger group, whose translated copies tile the whole group evenly — so the club's size must divide the total.
concepts.formal_gloss: A subset of a group closed under the operation and inverses; its cosets partition the group, whence Lagrange's theorem: the order of a subgroup divides the order of a finite group.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Homomorphism
concepts.intuition_gloss: A translation between two algebraic worlds that respects their grammar — combine then translate, or translate then combine, and you land in the same place.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map f between structures of the same signature with f(x * y) = f(x) * f(y) for each operation; kernels and images measure exactly what the map collapses and reaches.

concepts.name: Cyclic Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: A group cranked out by one repeating move — like the hour hand's twelve positions — the simplest engine of symmetry there is.
concepts.formal_gloss: A group generated by a single element, isomorphic to Z if infinite or to Z/nZ if of order n; every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Permutation & the Symmetric Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: All the ways to shuffle n objects, composed one after another — and secretly the mother of all groups, since every group is a group of shuffles in disguise.
concepts.formal_gloss: The group S_n of bijections of an n-element set under composition, of order n!; Cayley's theorem embeds every group in some symmetric group, and each permutation has a well-defined sign.

concepts.name: Normal Subgroup & Quotient Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: Deliberately blur a group by declaring a whole subgroup to count as 'nothing', and a smaller, coarser group emerges from the blur.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a subgroup N invariant under conjugation, the cosets of N form the group G/N; the first isomorphism theorem identifies G/ker(f) with the image of any homomorphism f.

concepts.name: Group Action
concepts.intuition_gloss: A group put to work moving the points of some object around; watching the orbits it carves out is how symmetry becomes a counting and classifying tool.
concepts.formal_gloss: A homomorphism from a group G to the permutations of a set X, written g·x; orbits partition X and the orbit-stabilizer theorem gives |orbit(x)| · |stab(x)| = |G|.

concepts.name: Sylow Theorems
concepts.intuition_gloss: Lagrange only says a subgroup's size must divide the group's; Sylow answers back — for the largest prime-power chunk of that size, a subgroup really does exist, all copies of it are just relabelings of each other, and you can even count how many there are.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a finite group of order p^n·m with p prime and p not dividing m: subgroups of order p^n (Sylow p-subgroups) exist, any two are conjugate, and their number divides m and is congruent to 1 mod p — proved via group actions on cosets and conjugates.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Ideal & Quotient Ring
concepts.intuition_gloss: The ring-world analogue of 'multiples of n': a subset that swallows multiplication, so you can mod out by it and do arithmetic with the remainders.
concepts.formal_gloss: An additive subgroup I of a ring R with rI ⊆ I for all r in R; the cosets form the quotient ring R/I, with I prime iff R/I is a domain and maximal iff R/I is a field.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Factorization in Rings (Euclidean, PID, UFD)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Asking which number-worlds keep the 'every number breaks uniquely into primes' promise — and meeting the famous worlds where the promise fails.
concepts.formal_gloss: The hierarchy Euclidean domain ⊂ principal ideal domain ⊂ unique factorization domain ⊂ integral domain, classifying rings by whether division, single-generator ideals, and unique irreducible factorization survive.

concepts.name: Field Extension
concepts.intuition_gloss: Growing a number system just enough to house a root it was missing — like adjoining sqrt(2) to the rationals — and measuring how much bigger the world got.
concepts.formal_gloss: A field K containing a field F, viewed as an F-vector space whose dimension [K:F] is the degree; elements algebraic over F have minimal polynomials, and splitting fields adjoin all roots of a given polynomial.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

concepts.name: Module
aka: structure theorem for finitely generated modules
concepts.intuition_gloss: A vector space whose scalars come from a mere ring instead of a field — a rougher habitat where scaling can crush elements to zero, as in clock arithmetic, unifying abelian groups and linear operators in one frame.
concepts.formal_gloss: An abelian group M with a ring action R × M → M satisfying the vector-space axioms over R; over a PID, finitely generated modules decompose into cyclic pieces, yielding both the classification of finite abelian groups and Jordan normal form.

concepts.name: Simple Groups & the Classification
concepts.intuition_gloss: The unsplittable atoms of symmetry — and the century-long census that found them all, ending in eighteen orderly families plus twenty-six bizarre outliers crowned by the Monster.
concepts.formal_gloss: A group whose only normal subgroups are trivial; the classification theorem states every finite simple group is cyclic of prime order, alternating, of Lie type, or one of 26 sporadic groups.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Group theory, abstraction, and the 196,883-dimensional monster
Wikipedia: Classification of finite simple groups

sources: Princeton Companion V.7 (Classification of Finite Simple Groups)

concepts.name: Galois Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Attach to each equation the group of ways its roots can be shuffled without breaking any algebraic relation — and read off from that group's shape why the quintic has no formula.
concepts.formal_gloss: The correspondence between intermediate fields of a Galois extension and subgroups of its automorphism group; a polynomial is solvable by radicals iff its Galois group is a solvable group.

resources.title: Abstract Algebra: Theory and Applications (Judson, free text)

sources: Princeton Companion V.21 (insolubility of the quintic)

concepts.name: Representation Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Turn every abstract symmetry into a concrete matrix, so the whole machinery of linear algebra can X-ray a group's internal anatomy.
concepts.formal_gloss: A homomorphism from a group to the invertible linear maps of a vector space; over C every finite-group representation decomposes into irreducibles, which characters classify completely.

resources.title: Introduction to Representation Theory (Etingof et al., MIT notes)

concepts.name: Lie Groups & Lie Algebras
concepts.intuition_gloss: Symmetries that vary smoothly, like all rotations of a sphere — studied by zooming in on the infinitesimal nudges near 'do nothing', which form an algebra of their own.
concepts.formal_gloss: A Lie group is a smooth manifold with smooth group operations; its tangent space at the identity carries a bilinear bracket [x,y] satisfying antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity, forming its Lie algebra.

concepts.name: Tensor Product
concepts.intuition_gloss: A machine that turns two-slot multiplication into one-slot linearity: build the freest possible space where a pair of vectors 'multiplies' to a single object, and every bilinear rule you could ever write factors through it.
concepts.formal_gloss: For modules M and N over a ring R, the module M ⊗_R N equipped with a universal bilinear map M × N → M ⊗ N through which every R-bilinear map factors uniquely; the construction from which the derived functor Tor arises.
sources: Evan Chen, An Infinitely Large Napkin (tensor product chapters)

concepts.name: Homological Algebra
concepts.intuition_gloss: Chains of maps where doing two steps in a row always gives zero; homology measures the leftover slack — things that die at the next step but didn't come from the previous one — and that slack detects the hidden holes and obstructions in an algebraic situation.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of chain complexes and exact sequences of modules, extracting invariants like homology, Ext, and Tor via derived functors in an abelian category.
sources: Princeton Companion (algebraic topology & modern algebra essays, IV.6)
Weibel, An Introduction to Homological Algebra (reference placement)
